subject: reading
title: Grade 5 Reading — EOG Practice Test 1
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions that follow. For each question, choose the best answer based on what the selection states or implies. Some questions may ask you to select two answers. You have 120 minutes to complete this test.

passages.title: The Last Tomato
passages.text:
Maya had checked the garden every single morning for three weeks, and every single morning the tomato vine offered her the same answer: not yet. The plant her grandmother had given her in May was tall now, taller than Maya herself, and it leaned against the wooden stake like a tired old man. Green tomatoes hung from it like little hard fists. Maya wanted, more than anything, to bring her grandmother one perfect red tomato before she moved away at the end of the summer.

"You can't rush a tomato," Grandma Rosa always said, laughing, her hands deep in the soil. "A tomato ripens when it is ready, not when you are ready." But Maya was not good at waiting. She measured the tomatoes with her fingers. She whispered to them. Once, when no one was watching, she even tried painting a green tomato red with a marker, until Grandma Rosa caught her and laughed so hard she had to sit down on the back steps.

Then came the storm. It arrived on a Thursday afternoon with no warning, slamming the screen door and bending the trees sideways. Maya watched from the kitchen window, her stomach twisting, as the wind shoved her tomato plant back and forth. When the rain finally stopped, she ran outside in her socks. The stake had snapped. The vine lay flat in the mud, and most of the tomatoes had been knocked loose and split open on the ground.

But one tomato remained. It hung at the very top of the broken vine, and as Maya knelt in the wet dirt, she saw that it had turned, overnight, a deep and glowing red. It was not a perfect tomato. One side was a little flat, and there was a small scar near the stem where a branch had scratched it. Maya cupped it in both hands as if it were made of glass.

That evening she carried it inside on a white plate and set it in front of her grandmother. Grandma Rosa looked at the lopsided, scarred tomato for a long moment. Then she looked at Maya, and her eyes were shining. "This," she said quietly, "is the most beautiful tomato I have ever seen." Maya started to explain about the storm, about how it wasn't perfect, but her grandmother shook her head. "The waiting is part of it," she said. "And so is the storm. That is how you know it is real."

Maya did not fully understand what her grandmother meant, not that night. But years later, long after Grandma Rosa had moved away and the garden had been planted with new things, Maya would stand at her own kitchen window during a storm, watching the wind move through a vine she had grown herself, and she would finally understand.

passages.title: How the Coyote Brought Fire
passages.text:
Long ago, the people of the valley had no fire. In the cold months they shivered in their homes, eating their food uncooked, watching their breath rise like smoke that gave no warmth. They knew that fire existed, for far away on the top of the great mountain lived three Fire Beings who guarded it jealously. The Fire Beings never slept all at once. One of them always kept watch over the flames, and they shrieked with anger whenever any creature came near.

Coyote, who was clever and curious and a little bit foolish, grew tired of watching the people suffer. "I will bring you fire," he announced, though no one had asked him to. The wise old woman of the village only shook her head. "Many have tried," she said. "None have returned." But Coyote had a plan, and Coyote's plans were always either very good or very bad, and sometimes both at once.

He climbed the great mountain and watched the Fire Beings for three days, hidden among the rocks. He noticed something the others had missed: at dawn, when the watch changed from one Fire Being to the next, there was a single moment when both were awake but neither was truly watching the fire. It was the smallest of openings. Coyote also knew he could not carry the fire alone, so he visited the other animals and asked each one to wait along the mountainside, ready to run.

When the gray light of dawn came, Coyote darted forward, seized a glowing branch in his teeth, and ran. The Fire Beings screamed and gave chase, their cries shaking loose stones from the mountain. Coyote ran until his legs burned, then flung the branch to Squirrel, who caught it on her back. The fire scorched her so badly that her tail curled up over her body, the way a squirrel's tail still curls today. Squirrel ran and passed the fire to Chipmunk, who was scratched by a Fire Being's claw, leaving three pale stripes down her back that her children wear to this day.

On and on the fire traveled, from animal to animal, each one carrying it a little farther and each one marked forever by the journey. At last Frog took the fire, but the Fire Beings were close, and they grabbed his tail. Frog gave a mighty leap and escaped, but his tail stayed behind in their hands, which is why frogs have no tails. Frog spat the fire into a tree, and the fire sank deep into the wood and hid there.

The Fire Beings searched and searched, but they could not pull the fire out of the tree, and at last they returned to their mountain in defeat. Then Coyote showed the people how to rub two dry sticks of that tree together until the hidden fire woke and leaped out. From that day on, the people had fire to warm their homes and cook their food. And though Coyote never asked for thanks, the people remembered, and they tell his story still.

passages.title: The Hidden World of Soil
passages.text:
When you walk across a field or a forest floor, you are stepping on one of the most crowded places on Earth. A single handful of healthy soil can contain more living organisms than there are people on the entire planet. Most of these living things are far too small to see, yet without them, life as we know it could not exist.

Soil is not simply dirt. It is a complex mixture of broken-down rock, water, air, and living and dead material. It forms slowly, over hundreds or even thousands of years, as rock is worn down by wind, water, and the roots of plants. Dead leaves, insects, and animals are mixed in and broken apart. The result is a living system that feeds the plants we depend on for food.

The most important workers in the soil are also some of the smallest. Bacteria and fungi break down dead plants and animals into nutrients that growing plants can absorb through their roots. Without these tiny decomposers, dead material would pile up endlessly, and the nutrients trapped inside it would never be returned to the soil. In this way, the smallest creatures make the largest forests possible.

Earthworms play a different but equally important role. As an earthworm tunnels through the ground, it swallows soil, digests the bits of dead material inside, and leaves behind waste that is rich in nutrients. Its tunnels also loosen the soil, allowing air and water to reach plant roots more easily. A single earthworm may seem unimportant, but a healthy field can contain a million earthworms in a single acre, and together they can move tons of soil each year.

Unfortunately, soil is in danger. When forests are cleared or farmland is left bare, rain and wind can carry away the rich top layer of soil, called topsoil. This process is known as erosion. Topsoil that took a thousand years to form can be washed away in a single season. Around the world, soil is being lost far faster than nature can replace it, and scientists warn that this loss threatens our ability to grow enough food.

The good news is that people are learning to protect the soil. Farmers can plant cover crops that hold the soil in place between growing seasons. They can plow less often, leaving the soil undisturbed and full of life. They can plant trees whose roots anchor the ground against wind and water. Protecting soil is not only about saving dirt. It is about protecting the hidden, crowded, living world beneath our feet, the world that quietly makes all other life possible.

passages.title: The Girl Who Mapped the Stars
passages.text:
In the late 1800s, the doors of science were closed to most women. Few were allowed to attend universities, and even fewer were given the chance to do real scientific work. Yet at the Harvard College Observatory, a remarkable group of women spent their days studying the stars, and one of them, Henrietta Swan Leavitt, would change the way humans measure the universe.

Leavitt was born in 1868 and loved astronomy from a young age. After college, she began working at the observatory, where she was hired as a "computer." This did not mean she worked with machines. In those days, a computer was a person who performed careful, repeated calculations by hand. Leavitt's job was to study photographs of the night sky and measure the brightness of thousands upon thousands of stars. It was slow, demanding work, and she was paid only a few cents an hour.

Leavitt became especially interested in a type of star that does not shine steadily but instead grows brighter and dimmer over a regular cycle. These stars are called variable stars. By comparing photographs taken on different nights, Leavitt patiently tracked how the brightness of each star changed over time. She measured more than a thousand of these variable stars, far more than anyone before her.

Then Leavitt noticed something that no one else had seen. Among a certain group of variable stars, the ones that were brighter took longer to complete their cycle of dimming and brightening. The dimmer stars cycled quickly; the brighter stars cycled slowly. The relationship was steady and predictable. This meant that if astronomers measured how long a star's cycle lasted, they could figure out how bright the star truly was, and from that, they could calculate how far away it was.

This discovery, known today as Leavitt's Law, gave astronomers their first reliable way to measure great distances in space. Before Leavitt, no one could say with confidence how far away the distant stars were. After her, astronomers had a kind of cosmic measuring stick. A few years later, a scientist named Edwin Hubble used Leavitt's discovery to prove that the universe is far larger than anyone had imagined, containing countless galaxies beyond our own.

Henrietta Leavitt did not live to receive the honors she deserved. She died in 1921, and much of the credit for the discoveries her work made possible went to the men who came after her. Yet today, scientists recognize that the modern understanding of the universe rests, in part, on the patient work of a woman who counted the changing light of the stars, one photograph at a time.

passages.title: The Old Oak
passages.text:
I have stood here a hundred years,
my roots like fingers gripping stone,
and I have watched the seasons turn
while children grew and then were grown.

In spring I wear a coat of green,
in summer, shade for those below—
the farmer resting in my arms,
the lovers carving names I know.

The autumn sets my leaves on fire,
a thousand torches, gold and red,
that one by one let go their hold
and make a blanket for my bed.

Then winter strips me to my bones.
I stand alone against the sky,
but I am not afraid of cold—
I know that spring is drawing nigh.

The lightning split my oldest branch;
the storms have bent me, year by year.
And yet I am not made of fear—
I am made of standing here.

So when the small ones ask me how
a tree can live so long, so tall,
I tell them: bend, but do not break,
and answer green to winter's call.

questions.questionText: What is a theme of "The Last Tomato"?
options: Grandparents know more about gardening than children do.
True value comes from waiting through hardship, not from perfection.
It is foolish to grow tomatoes in a place that has storms.
Storms always destroy the things we work hardest to grow.
questions.explanation: Grandma Rosa says the waiting and the storm are 'how you know it is real,' and Maya later understands this, showing the theme that worth comes from patience and struggle rather than perfection. The scarred tomato being called 'the most beautiful' supports this.

questions.questionText: From whose point of view is the story told?
options: The tomato plant's, in the first person
Maya's, through a narrator outside the story
Grandma Rosa's, in the first person
A neighbor who watches Maya's garden
questions.explanation: The story is told by a third-person narrator who follows Maya's thoughts and feelings ('her stomach twisting,' 'Maya did not fully understand'), so events are seen mainly through Maya's perspective but not in her own voice.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from the passage: "Green tomatoes hung from it like little hard fists." What does this comparison suggest about the tomatoes?
options: They were angry at Maya for rushing them.
They were still tight, unripe, and not ready.
They were so heavy they pulled the vine down.
They were soft and ready to be picked.
questions.explanation: Comparing the green tomatoes to 'little hard fists' emphasizes that they are closed up, hard, and unripe — matching the plant's repeated answer of 'not yet.'

questions.questionText: Which detail best supports the idea that Maya was impatient?
options: She carried the tomato inside on a white plate.
She tried painting a green tomato red with a marker.
She checked the garden every morning for three weeks.
She watched the storm from the kitchen window.
questions.explanation: Trying to paint a green tomato red shows Maya could not wait for it to ripen naturally, directly supporting that she was impatient. Checking daily shows interest, but painting it red shows impatience most clearly.

questions.questionText: How does Maya change from the beginning of the story to the end?
options: She moves from rushing things to understanding the value of waiting.
She moves from enjoying gardening to disliking it.
She moves from loving her grandmother to forgetting her.
She moves from fearing storms to causing them.
questions.explanation: At the start Maya rushes the tomatoes and cannot wait; by the end she 'finally understands' what her grandmother meant about waiting and storms, showing her growth.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence: "Maya cupped it in both hands as if it were made of glass." What does the phrase "as if it were made of glass" suggest about how Maya felt about the tomato?
options: She was afraid the tomato would cut her.
She believed the tomato was fake.
She thought the tomato was cold and smooth.
She treated the tomato as precious and fragile.
questions.explanation: Holding something 'as if it were made of glass' means handling it gently and carefully because it is precious and breakable — showing how much the tomato meant to Maya.

questions.questionText: Why does Grandma Rosa call the scarred, lopsided tomato "the most beautiful tomato I have ever seen"?
options: She has never seen a red tomato before.
She values the love and struggle behind it more than its looks.
She cannot see the tomato's flaws clearly.
She is teasing Maya for bringing an imperfect tomato.
questions.explanation: Grandma Rosa explains that 'the waiting is part of it' and 'so is the storm,' showing she values the effort and meaning behind the tomato, not its appearance.

questions.questionText: Select two details that show the storm was an important turning point in the story.
options: Grandma Rosa gave Maya the plant in May.
Maya whispered to the tomatoes when no one watched.
The stake snapped and the vine lay flat in the mud.
The one remaining tomato had turned deep red overnight.
Maya measured the tomatoes with her fingers.
questions.explanation: The storm breaks the vine (option 1) and yet leaves one tomato that has finally ripened red (option 3) — together these mark the turning point that gives Maya her gift. The other options describe events before the storm.

questions.questionText: What is a theme of "How the Coyote Brought Fire"?
options: It is dangerous to climb tall mountains.
Cleverness alone is enough to solve any problem.
Animals should never trust one another.
Helping others often requires sacrifice and working together.
questions.explanation: Coyote cannot bring fire alone; many animals carry it and are each marked or hurt in the effort. The story's theme centers on sacrifice and cooperation to help the people.

questions.questionText: How does Coyote's plan differ from the attempts of those who tried before him?
options: He waits for the brief moment at dawn and gets help from other animals.
He fights the Fire Beings instead of running from them.
He asks the wise old woman to steal the fire for him.
He climbs the mountain at night while the Fire Beings sleep.
questions.explanation: Coyote notices the single moment at dawn when neither Fire Being is truly watching, and he arranges for other animals to relay the fire — strategies the failed attempts lacked.

questions.questionText: This legend mainly explains —
options: how certain animals got their physical features and how people got fire
how the valley people learned to farm
why the Fire Beings never sleep
why coyotes live near mountains
questions.explanation: The story explains the squirrel's curled tail, the chipmunk's stripes, the frog's missing tail, and how people gained fire — classic features of an origin legend.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from the passage: "It was the smallest of openings." What does the word "openings" mean as it is used here?
options: a chance or opportunity to act
a hole in the mountainside
a doorway into the Fire Beings' home
the beginning of a story
questions.explanation: Here 'opening' is used figuratively to mean a brief chance or opportunity — the moment when neither Fire Being is watching the fire.

questions.questionText: According to the legend, why do frogs have no tails?
options: Frog burned his tail off carrying the fire.
Coyote took Frog's tail as payment for the fire.
Frog traded his tail to the tree for safety.
A Fire Being grabbed Frog's tail, and it stayed behind when Frog leaped away.
questions.explanation: The text states that the Fire Beings grabbed Frog's tail, and when Frog leaped away 'his tail stayed behind in their hands, which is why frogs have no tails.'

questions.questionText: The narrator says Coyote's plans "were always either very good or very bad, and sometimes both at once." What does this statement help the reader understand about Coyote?
options: Coyote usually fails at whatever he tries.
Coyote does not care whether the people get fire.
Coyote is too cautious to ever take action.
Coyote is unpredictable and takes risks that can succeed or backfire.
questions.explanation: Describing his plans as both very good and very bad shows Coyote is a risk-taker whose bold ideas can succeed or go wrong — fitting his clever-but-foolish nature.

questions.questionText: How might the story be different if it were told from the point of view of one of the Fire Beings?
options: The animals would not receive any markings.
The people would never get fire.
Coyote would be shown as a thief who steals what they guard.
Squirrel would be the hero of the story.
questions.explanation: From the Fire Beings' point of view, Coyote — the hero in this version — would appear as a thief taking the fire they jealously guard, reversing the reader's sympathy.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "The Hidden World of Soil"?
options: Earthworms are the only living things found in soil.
Farmers are responsible for destroying the world's soil.
Soil takes a thousand years to form and cannot be saved.
Soil is a living system full of organisms that supports life and must be protected.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that soil is full of living organisms, supports the plants we depend on, is threatened by erosion, and can be protected — making this the central idea.

questions.questionText: How are the fifth and sixth paragraphs (about erosion and about protecting soil) organized?
options: They list events in the order they happened in history.
They compare two different kinds of soil.
They describe a process in numbered steps.
They present a problem and then offer solutions.
questions.explanation: Paragraph five describes the problem (soil erosion and loss), and paragraph six describes solutions (cover crops, less plowing, planting trees) — a problem-and-solution structure.

questions.questionText: Based on the passage, what is the relationship between bacteria and the growth of large forests?
options: Bacteria break down dead material into nutrients that plants need to grow.
Bacteria compete with trees for nutrients, slowing forest growth.
Bacteria block water from reaching the roots of trees.
Bacteria are eaten by earthworms, which then feed the trees.
questions.explanation: The text states bacteria and fungi break down dead plants and animals into nutrients plants absorb, so 'the smallest creatures make the largest forests possible.'

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "decomposers" as it is used in the third paragraph?
options: tools that farmers use to dig
living things that break down dead material
plants that grow in poor soil
animals that eat living plants
questions.explanation: The paragraph calls bacteria and fungi 'tiny decomposers' right after explaining that they 'break down dead plants and animals,' giving the meaning in context.

questions.questionText: According to the passage, what is topsoil?
options: the waste left behind by earthworms
a kind of cover crop planted by farmers
the rich top layer of soil that can be carried away by erosion
the deepest layer of rock beneath the soil
questions.explanation: The passage defines topsoil as 'the rich top layer of soil' that rain and wind can carry away during erosion.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence: "In this way, the smallest creatures make the largest forests possible." Why does the author contrast "smallest" and "largest" in this sentence?
options: to show that forests are shrinking over time
to argue that small creatures are more important than trees
to emphasize how tiny organisms have an enormous effect
to explain why bacteria grow into large plants
questions.explanation: Contrasting 'smallest' with 'largest' highlights the surprising idea that microscopic organisms make huge forests possible — emphasizing their outsized importance.

questions.questionText: Select two ways earthworms help the soil, according to the passage.
options: They dig up topsoil so wind can carry it away.
They plant cover crops between growing seasons.
They eat bacteria and fungi to control them.
They leave behind waste that is rich in nutrients.
Their tunnels loosen the soil so air and water reach roots.
questions.explanation: The text says earthworm waste is 'rich in nutrients' (option 1) and that their tunnels 'loosen the soil, allowing air and water to reach plant roots' (option 3). The other options are not supported.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the passage best supports the idea that soil is fragile and in danger?
options: "A single earthworm may seem unimportant."
"Soil is a complex mixture of broken-down rock, water, air, and living and dead material."
"Soil is not simply dirt."
"Topsoil that took a thousand years to form can be washed away in a single season."
questions.explanation: The contrast between a thousand years to form and a single season to be washed away most directly shows soil is fragile and threatened.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "The Girl Who Mapped the Stars"?
options: Henrietta Leavitt's patient work gave astronomers a way to measure distances in space.
Women in the 1800s were not allowed to attend universities.
Edwin Hubble discovered that the universe is full of galaxies.
Variable stars grow brighter and dimmer over a regular cycle.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on how Leavitt's careful study of variable stars produced Leavitt's Law, giving astronomers their first reliable way to measure great distances.

questions.questionText: In the second paragraph, the author explains that a "computer" was a person, not a machine. Why does the author include this explanation?
options: to explain why Leavitt was paid so little
to prove that machines were better than people at math
to prevent readers from misunderstanding the word's older meaning
to show that Leavitt invented the first computer
questions.explanation: Because readers today think of a machine, the author clarifies that in that era a 'computer' was a person doing calculations by hand, preventing confusion.

questions.questionText: What was the key relationship Leavitt discovered among the variable stars?
options: Closer stars were always dimmer than distant stars.
All variable stars cycled at exactly the same speed.
Brighter stars took longer to complete their cycle than dimmer stars.
Stars that cycled quickly were always the largest.
questions.explanation: Leavitt noticed that brighter variable stars 'took longer to complete their cycle,' while dimmer ones cycled quickly — a steady, predictable relationship.

questions.questionText: How is the passage as a whole organized?
options: by describing the same event from several points of view
in time order, following Leavitt's life and discovery
by listing problems with the Harvard Observatory
by comparing Leavitt to Edwin Hubble throughout
questions.explanation: The passage moves chronologically from Leavitt's birth and early love of astronomy, through her work and discovery, to her death and lasting legacy.

questions.questionText: Which detail best supports the idea that Leavitt did not receive the recognition she deserved?
options: Much of the credit for her discoveries went to the men who came after her.
She loved astronomy from a young age.
She was hired at the Harvard College Observatory as a computer.
She measured more than a thousand variable stars.
questions.explanation: The statement that credit 'went to the men who came after her' directly supports the idea that Leavitt did not get the recognition she earned.

questions.questionText: The passage describes Leavitt's discovery as "a kind of cosmic measuring stick." What does this phrase mean?
options: a real ruler used to measure telescopes
a method for measuring enormous distances in space
a tool astronomers built out of metal
a stick used to point at stars in photographs
questions.explanation: A 'cosmic measuring stick' is a figurative phrase meaning Leavitt's law gave astronomers a reliable way to measure great distances in space.

questions.questionText: What was the relationship between Leavitt's discovery and Edwin Hubble's work?
options: Hubble trained Leavitt to study variable stars.
Hubble used Leavitt's discovery to show the universe contains countless galaxies.
Leavitt and Hubble made the same discovery at the same time.
Hubble's work proved that Leavitt's discovery was wrong.
questions.explanation: The text states that Hubble 'used Leavitt's discovery to prove that the universe is far larger than anyone had imagined, containing countless galaxies.'

questions.questionText: What is a theme of the poem "The Old Oak"?
options: Endurance comes from being flexible and patient through hard times.
Children should not carve their names into trees.
Trees are stronger than any storm or lightning.
Winter is the most dangerous season for living things.
questions.explanation: The oak survives storms and lightning by enduring and trusting that spring will return, and it advises the young to 'bend, but do not break' — a theme of patient endurance.

questions.questionText: From whose point of view is the poem told?
options: a farmer resting beneath the tree
a narrator watching the seasons change
a child asking the tree questions
the oak tree itself
questions.explanation: The poem is spoken in the first person by the oak tree ('I have stood here a hundred years,' 'my roots'), giving the tree its own voice.

questions.questionText: Read these lines: "The autumn sets my leaves on fire, / a thousand torches, gold and red." What do these lines mean?
options: The tree gives off heat and light in autumn.
Someone lit torches in the tree's branches.
The leaves turn bright gold and red colors in the fall.
The tree was actually burned in a fire during autumn.
questions.explanation: This is figurative language: comparing the autumn leaves to fire and torches describes their bright gold and red colors, not a real fire.

questions.questionText: Read this line: "I am made of standing here." What does this line suggest about the oak?
options: The tree wishes it could stand somewhere else.
The tree cannot move because its roots are stuck.
The tree's strength and identity come from enduring in one place over time.
The tree is made of wood that came from this place.
questions.explanation: Saying it is 'made of standing here' rather than 'made of fear' means the oak's character and strength come from having endured in one place through everything.

questions.questionText: How does the structure of the poem, moving through spring, summer, autumn, and winter, help convey its message?
options: It shows the tree enduring through a full cycle of change and still standing.
It shows that the tree changes into a different tree each season.
It shows that winter is the only season that matters.
It lists the seasons in the order the speaker likes them.
questions.explanation: By moving through all four seasons, the poem shows the oak surviving constant change and remaining standing, reinforcing its message of endurance.

questions.questionText: In the line "my roots like fingers gripping stone," the oak's roots are compared to fingers. What does this comparison suggest?
options: The roots are reaching up toward the sky.
The roots hold tightly and firmly to the ground.
The roots are soft and weak.
The roots are shaped exactly like human hands.
questions.explanation: Comparing roots to fingers 'gripping stone' suggests they hold firmly and tightly to the earth, emphasizing the tree's stability.

questions.questionText: Both "The Old Oak" and "The Last Tomato" deal with surviving storms. How do the two texts present storms differently?
options: In the poem the storm is avoided, while in the story it never arrives.
In the poem storms shape the oak over many years, while in the story one storm brings a single turning point.
In the poem the storm destroys the oak, while in the story the storm saves Maya's whole garden.
Both texts treat the storm as a small, unimportant event.
questions.explanation: In the poem, repeated storms over years bend and shape the oak ('the storms have bent me, year by year'); in the story, a single sudden storm is the turning point that ripens the one tomato. This is the key difference.

questions.questionText: The word "erosion" contains a root meaning "to wear away." Based on this and the passage, what does "erosion" most nearly mean?
options: the digging of tunnels by earthworms
the planting of trees to protect the land
the slow forming of new soil over time
the wearing away and carrying off of soil by wind and water
questions.explanation: Using the root 'to wear away' plus the passage's description of rain and wind carrying away topsoil, erosion means the wearing away and loss of soil.

questions.questionText: In the passage, the word "variable" is used to describe stars that grow brighter and dimmer. The prefix and root of "variable" relate to "change." What does "variable" most nearly mean?
options: located far away
able to change or vary
extremely bright
very large in size
questions.explanation: 'Variable' shares its root with 'vary,' meaning to change; the passage confirms variable stars change in brightness over a cycle.

questions.questionText: In the first paragraph, the tomato vine "leaned against the wooden stake like a tired old man." What does this figurative comparison suggest?
options: The vine was actually as old as an elderly person.
The vine was angry and ready to fall over on purpose.
The vine belonged to an old man in the neighborhood.
The vine was heavy and drooping, needing support to stay up.
questions.explanation: Comparing the vine to 'a tired old man' leaning on the stake suggests it was heavy and weary, needing the stake's support to stay upright.
